title: Dog Walking with Arched Back and Head Low: Triage and Emergency Guide
meta_desc: If your dog is walking with an arched back and head low, they may be in severe pain. Learn why this is a high-urgency veterinary emergency.
content_html:
TL;DR: An arched back and low head in dogs are signs of severe pain or neurological distress requiring immediate emergency veterinary care. This posture often indicates spinal cord pressure or serious internal issues that can lead to permanent damage if left untreated.

What does it mean if my dog has an arched back and holds their head low?

When a dog walks with an arched back (kyphosis) and keeps their head tucked low, it is a significant clinical sign of acute pain or neurological distress used to guard the spine or abdomen. Common causes include:

  • Intervertebral Disc Disease (IVDD)
  • Spinal trauma or neck injuries
  • Severe internal abdominal pain, such as pancreatitis

Is an arched back and low head posture a veterinary emergency?

Urgency Level: High. You should contact an emergency veterinarian immediately because this posture often indicates the spinal cord is under pressure or there is a serious internal medical issue. Immediate intervention is necessary if the dog shows:

  • Shivering or shaking
  • Reluctance to move
  • Weakness in the limbs
  • Signs of worsening systemic crisis

How can taking photos or videos of my dog help the veterinarian?

Dogs often experience an adrenaline spike at the clinic that masks their pain, making visual evidence from home vital for an accurate assessment. If it is safe to do so, prepare the following for your veterinary team:

  • A quick video of your dog walking
  • A clear photo of their standing posture
  • Visuals of the behavior occurring in a relaxed home environment

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

While the provided excerpts do not directly address a dog walking with an arched back and head low, clinical signs suggestive of underlying conditions are discussed. In cases of suspected polyradiculoneuritis, dogs may appear hyperesthetic, showing signs of discomfort on palpation of the trunk or limbs, although mentation, appetite, urination, defecation, and tail movement usually remain normal. Tetanus can cause localized or generalized muscle stiffness and extensor rigidity, potentially leading to opisthotonos and reflex muscle spasms. Arterial thromboembolism can cause acute, painful paraparesis with an inability to flex or extend the hock, decreased sensation distal to the hock, and weak or absent femoral pulses.

Chapter: Neurology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1240)

faqs.question: Can I give my dog aspirin for the pain?
faqs.answer: No. Never give human pain medications to a dog without veterinary approval, as many are toxic and can complicate further treatment or cause stomach ulcers.

faqs.question: Should I encourage my dog to walk it off?
faqs.answer: No. If a spinal injury is present, movement can worsen the condition. Keep your dog as still as possible in a crate or small area until you reach the vet.

faqs.question: Is this behavior common in certain breeds?
faqs.answer: While it can happen to any dog, breeds with long backs and short legs, such as Dachshunds, Corgis, and Basset Hounds, are at a higher risk for spinal issues like IVDD.
